([/ˈpɛʃi/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), Italian: [[ˈpeʃʃi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); born February 9, 1943) is an American actor. He is best known for portraying tough, volatile characters in a variety of genres and for his collaborations with [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese") and [Robert De Niro](/wiki/Robert_De_Niro "Robert De Niro") in the films *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* (1980), *[Goodfellas](/wiki/Goodfellas "Goodfellas")* (1990), *[Casino](/wiki/Casino_(1995_film) "Casino (1995 film)")* (1995), and *[The Irishman](/wiki/The_Irishman "The Irishman")* (2019).

**Joseph Frank Pesci** ([/ˈpɛʃi/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), Italian: [[ˈpeʃʃi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian"); born February 9, 1943) is an American actor. He is best known for portraying tough, volatile characters in a variety of genres and for his collaborations with [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese") and [Robert De Niro](/wiki/Robert_De_Niro "Robert De Niro") in the films *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* (1980), *[Goodfellas](/wiki/Goodfellas "Goodfellas")* (1990), *[Casino](/wiki/Casino_(1995_film) "Casino (1995 film)")* (1995), and *[The Irishman](/wiki/The_Irishman "The Irishman")* (2019).

Pesci also appeared in *[Once Upon a Time in America](/wiki/Once_Upon_a_Time_in_America "Once Upon a Time in America")* (1984), *[Moonwalker](/wiki/Moonwalker "Moonwalker")* (1988), *[JFK](/wiki/JFK_(film) "JFK (film)")* (1991), *[A Bronx Tale](/wiki/A_Bronx_Tale "A Bronx Tale")* (1993), and *[The Good Shepherd](/wiki/The_Good_Shepherd_(film) "The Good Shepherd (film)")* (2006). He is also known for his comic roles in *[Home Alone](/wiki/Home_Alone "Home Alone")* (1990) and [its sequel](/wiki/Home_Alone_2:_Lost_in_New_York "Home Alone 2: Lost in New York") (1992), *[My Cousin Vinny](/wiki/My_Cousin_Vinny "My Cousin Vinny")* (1992), and the [*Lethal Weapon* franchise](/wiki/Lethal_Weapon_(franchise) "Lethal Weapon (franchise)") (1989–1998).

Pesci won the [Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor") for his role as the gangster character [Tommy DeVito](/wiki/Thomas_DeSimone#Portrayal_in_film "Thomas DeSimone") in *Goodfellas* and received two other nominations in the same category for his portrayals of [Joey LaMotta](/wiki/Joey_LaMotta "Joey LaMotta") and [Russell Bufalino](/wiki/Russell_Bufalino "Russell Bufalino") in *Raging Bull* and *The Irishman*, respectively. Pesci retired from acting in 1999, but has periodically returned to act since then.

Pesci is also a musician who has recorded three studio albums: *Little Joe Sure Can Sing!* (1968), *[Vincent LaGuardia Gambini Sings Just for You](/wiki/Vincent_LaGuardia_Gambini_Sings_Just_for_You "Vincent LaGuardia Gambini Sings Just for You")* (1998), and *Pesci... Still Singing* (2019).

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joe_Pesci&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Joseph Frank Pesci was born on February 9, 1943, in Newark, New Jersey. His mother, Mary (née Mesce), was a part-time barber, and his father, Angelo Pesci, was a [forklift](/wiki/Forklift "Forklift") truck driver for [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors") and a bartender. Pesci is of [Italian](/wiki/Italian_Americans "Italian Americans") descent with family origins both in [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin")[[1]](#cite_note-character-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) and [Aquilonia](/wiki/Aquilonia,_Campania "Aquilonia, Campania") in the [province of Avellino](/wiki/Province_of_Avellino "Province of Avellino").[[4]](#cite_note-4) He was raised in [Belleville, New Jersey](/wiki/Belleville,_New_Jersey "Belleville, New Jersey"), and graduated from [Belleville High School](/wiki/Belleville_High_School_(New_Jersey) "Belleville High School (New Jersey)").

By the time Pesci was five years old, he was appearing in plays in New York.[[1]](#cite_note-character-1) At age 10, Pesci was a regular on a television variety show called *Startime Kids*, which also featured [Connie Francis](/wiki/Connie_Francis "Connie Francis").[[1]](#cite_note-character-1) As an entertainer from Belleville, he was acquainted with guitarist [Tommy DeVito](/wiki/Tommy_DeVito_(musician) "Tommy DeVito (musician)") of [The Four Lovers](/wiki/The_Four_Lovers "The Four Lovers"), a novelty act from Belleville that included singer [Frankie Valli](/wiki/Frankie_Valli "Frankie Valli"). Pesci also knew keyboardist [Bob Gaudio](/wiki/Bob_Gaudio "Bob Gaudio") of [The Royal Teens](/wiki/The_Royal_Teens "The Royal Teens"). Pesci introduced Gaudio to DeVito and Valli in 1958, which led to the formation of the band [The Four Seasons](/wiki/The_Four_Seasons_(band) "The Four Seasons (band)").[[5]](#cite_note-history_vs_hollywood-5) Pesci and DeVito remained friends for the rest of DeVito's life; when DeVito fell on hard times in the 1970s following his resignation from the Four Seasons, Pesci placed DeVito on his personal payroll and arranged for him to make cameos in some of his films in the 1990s.[[6]](#cite_note-devitolvinterview-6)

Pesci was childhood friends with [American Mafia](/wiki/American_Mafia "American Mafia") figure Robert Bisaccia.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joe_Pesci&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Early career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joe_Pesci&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early career")]

In the 1960s, Pesci began working as a barber. At the same time, Pesci tried to start a musical career, playing guitar with several bands, including [Joey Dee and the Starliters](/wiki/Joey_Dee_and_the_Starliters "Joey Dee and the Starliters"), who introduced the "[Peppermint Twist](/wiki/Peppermint_Twist "Peppermint Twist")" record, dance, and Peppermint Lounge in New York City.

In 1968, Pesci released his debut album *Little Joe Sure Can Sing!* (billed as *Joe Ritchie*), on which he sang covers of contemporary pop hits.[[8]](#cite_note-yahoo-8)

Pesci later joined [Frank Vincent](/wiki/Frank_Vincent "Frank Vincent") as a [comedy duo](/wiki/Double_act "Double act"), performing as "Vincent and Pesci" from 1970 to 1976. Their act coupled [Abbott and Costello](/wiki/Abbott_and_Costello "Abbott and Costello")-inspired double act antics with [Don Rickles](/wiki/Don_Rickles "Don Rickles")-style [insult comedy](/wiki/Insult_comedy "Insult comedy"), which proved popular with crowds. During this time, the pair developed a strong professional and personal friendship.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) In 1975, they appeared in the Broadway show *The New Vaudevillians*, which only lasted a week.[[8]](#cite_note-yahoo-8)

The first film Pesci starred in was the 1976 [low-budget](/wiki/Low-budget_film "Low-budget film") [crime film](/wiki/Crime_film "Crime film") *[The Death Collector](/wiki/The_Death_Collector "The Death Collector")* alongside Frank Vincent. After the film, Pesci returned to The Bronx and lived above Amici's Restaurant, where he was an employee.[[8]](#cite_note-yahoo-8)

### Acting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joe_Pesci&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Acting")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Cathy_Moriarty_and_Joe_Pesci%2C_1980.jpg/250px-Cathy_Moriarty_and_Joe_Pesci%2C_1980.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cathy_Moriarty_and_Joe_Pesci,_1980.jpg)

Pesci with co-star [Cathy Moriarty](/wiki/Cathy_Moriarty "Cathy Moriarty") on the set of *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")*

In 1979, Pesci received a phone call from [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese") and Robert De Niro, who were impressed with his performance in *The Death Collector* and asked him to co-star in Scorsese's *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* as [Joey LaMotta](/wiki/Joey_LaMotta "Joey LaMotta"). During the course of filming, Pesci broke one of his ribs during a fight scene with De Niro.[[11]](#cite_note-LATimes-11) For his role in the film, he was nominated for an [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") for [Best Supporting Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor#1980s "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor") and won [Most Promising Newcomer](/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Most_Promising_Newcomer_to_Leading_Film_Roles#1980s "BAFTA Award for Most Promising Newcomer to Leading Film Roles") at the 1981 British [British Academy Film Awards](/wiki/British_Academy_Film_Awards "British Academy Film Awards").

In 1982, according to a conversation intercepted by the [FBI](/wiki/FBI "FBI") between actor [James Caan](/wiki/James_Caan "James Caan") and mobster [Anthony Fiato](/wiki/Anthony_Fiato "Anthony Fiato"), Caan requested that Fiato beat up Pesci for failing to pay an $8,000 hotel bill.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In 1984, Pesci was cast in *[Once Upon a Time in America](/wiki/Once_Upon_a_Time_in_America "Once Upon a Time in America")*, again appearing alongside De Niro. The following year, Pesci starred as private detective Rocky Nelson in the short-lived television comedy series *[Half Nelson](/wiki/Half_Nelson_(TV_series) "Half Nelson (TV series)")*.[[8]](#cite_note-yahoo-8)

In 1988, Pesci appeared in the [Michael Jackson](/wiki/Michael_Jackson "Michael Jackson") musical anthology film *[Moonwalker](/wiki/Moonwalker "Moonwalker")*, in the film's sixth and longest segment, "[Smooth Criminal](/wiki/Smooth_Criminal "Smooth Criminal")". He played the antagonist, crime boss Frankie "Mr. Big" LiDeo (an anagram for one of the film's producers and longtime Jackson manager [Frank DiLeo](/wiki/Frank_DiLeo "Frank DiLeo"),[[14]](#cite_note-14) with whom Pesci later acted in *Goodfellas*).[[15]](#cite_note-oscars1-15)

Pesci appeared as Leo Getz, a comedic sidekick and best friend to protagonist detectives Martin Riggs ([Mel Gibson](/wiki/Mel_Gibson "Mel Gibson")) and Roger Murtaugh ([Danny Glover](/wiki/Danny_Glover "Danny Glover")) in the *[Lethal Weapon](/wiki/Lethal_Weapon_(franchise) "Lethal Weapon (franchise)")* sequels, released in [1989](/wiki/Lethal_Weapon_2 "Lethal Weapon 2"), [1992](/wiki/Lethal_Weapon_3 "Lethal Weapon 3"), and [1998](/wiki/Lethal_Weapon_4 "Lethal Weapon 4").

In 1990, Pesci reunited with Scorsese and De Niro for *[Goodfellas](/wiki/Goodfellas "Goodfellas")*, where he played mobster Tommy DeVito, based on real-life mobster [Thomas DeSimone](/wiki/Thomas_DeSimone "Thomas DeSimone"). Tommy DeVito was also the name of Pesci's old acquaintance from Belleville, New Jersey, and a member of The Four Seasons, but contrary to popular belief, the naming is coincidental.[[5]](#cite_note-history_vs_hollywood-5) Pesci's old friend [Frank Vincent](/wiki/Frank_Vincent "Frank Vincent") also appears in the film. Pesci's character kills Vincent's character in a rage in one of the best-remembered scenes in the film after the Vincent character contemptuously tells him to "go home and get your fucking shine box". According to Pesci, improvisation and ad-libbing came out of rehearsals, where Scorsese let the actors do whatever they wanted. He made transcripts of these sessions, took the lines the actors came up with that he liked best, and put them into a revised script that the cast worked from during principal photography.[[16]](#cite_note-Arnold-16)

For example, the scene where Tommy tells a story and Henry is responding to him—the "Funny how? Do I amuse you?" scene—is based on an actual event that Pesci experienced. He was waiting tables when he thought he made a compliment to a mobster by saying he was "funny", which was not taken well.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) It was worked on in rehearsals where he and Liotta improvised, and Scorsese recorded four to five takes, rewrote their dialogue, and inserted it into the script.[[19]](#cite_note-Kaplan-19) The dinner scene with Tommy's mother was largely improvised.[[20]](#cite_note-Godfrey-20) Pesci received the [Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor") for the role, which he accepted with one of the shortest speeches in Oscar history, saying simply, "It's my privilege. Thank you" before leaving the stage.[[15]](#cite_note-oscars1-15)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Pesci also co-starred in the blockbuster *[Home Alone](/wiki/Home_Alone "Home Alone")* in 1990, his character Harry Lime one of two bumbling burglars - along with [Daniel Stern](/wiki/Daniel_Stern_(actor) "Daniel Stern (actor)") as Marv Murchins - who attempt to burgle the house of an eight-year-old played by [Macaulay Culkin](/wiki/Macaulay_Culkin "Macaulay Culkin"). Pesci's use of "cartoon cursing" or menacing gibberish, garnered comparisons to *[Looney Tunes](/wiki/Looney_Tunes "Looney Tunes")* character [Yosemite Sam](/wiki/Yosemite_Sam "Yosemite Sam").[[22]](#cite_note-22) Pesci reprised his role in a sequel *[Home Alone 2: Lost in New York](/wiki/Home_Alone_2:_Lost_in_New_York "Home Alone 2: Lost in New York")* (1992).

In 1991, Pesci played [David Ferrie](/wiki/David_Ferrie "David Ferrie") in *[JFK](/wiki/JFK_(film) "JFK (film)")*. The following year, he appeared as the title character in the comedy *[My Cousin Vinny](/wiki/My_Cousin_Vinny "My Cousin Vinny")* with [Ralph Macchio](/wiki/Ralph_Macchio "Ralph Macchio"), [Marisa Tomei](/wiki/Marisa_Tomei "Marisa Tomei") and [Fred Gwynne](/wiki/Fred_Gwynne "Fred Gwynne"). Also in 1992, Pesci headed up the cast of *[The Public Eye](/wiki/The_Public_Eye_(film) "The Public Eye (film)")* as Leon "Bernzy" Bernstein, a photographer. His performance in the film, a departure from his usual characters, has been critically acclaimed.

Pesci hosted the sketch comedy show *[Saturday Night Live](/wiki/Saturday_Night_Live "Saturday Night Live")* on October 10, 1992, while doing publicity for *My Cousin Vinny*. During his monologue, in response to [Sinéad O'Connor](/wiki/Sin%C3%A9ad_O%27Connor "Sinéad O'Connor") [tearing a picture](/wiki/Sin%C3%A9ad_O%27Connor_on_Saturday_Night_Live "Sinéad O'Connor on Saturday Night Live") of [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II") on the previous broadcast in protest of sexual abuse in the Catholic Church, Pesci described how he wished to give her "such a smack".[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In 1993, Pesci made an appearance in *[A Bronx Tale](/wiki/A_Bronx_Tale "A Bronx Tale")* as Carmine. The film starred Robert De Niro, who also directed, and [Chazz Palminteri](/wiki/Chazz_Palminteri "Chazz Palminteri"), who wrote the play from which the film was adapted. Both De Niro and Palminteri personally offered Pesci the role. In 1995, Pesci had his third collaboration with Scorsese and De Niro in the film *[Casino](/wiki/Casino_(1995_film) "Casino (1995 film)")*, playing Nicky Santoro, based on real-life Mob enforcer [Anthony Spilotro](/wiki/Anthony_Spilotro "Anthony Spilotro"), along with [Sharon Stone](/wiki/Sharon_Stone "Sharon Stone") and [James Woods](/wiki/James_Woods "James Woods"). Pesci had previously co-starred with the latter in *Once Upon a Time in America*. During filming, Pesci broke the same rib that had been broken 15 years prior during the production of *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")*.[[11]](#cite_note-LATimes-11) In 1996, he was considered to play Myron Larabee, the stressed-out postman, in *[Jingle All the Way](/wiki/Jingle_All_the_Way "Jingle All the Way")* opposite [Arnold Schwarzenegger](/wiki/Arnold_Schwarzenegger "Arnold Schwarzenegger"), but the part was ultimately given to [Sinbad](/wiki/Sinbad_(entertainer) "Sinbad (entertainer)"), whose physical size was more comparable to Schwarzenegger's.

Pesci had starring roles in several other films, including *[Man on Fire](/wiki/Man_on_Fire_(1987_film) "Man on Fire (1987 film)")* (1987), *[The Super](/wiki/The_Super_(1991_film) "The Super (1991 film)")* (1991), *[Jimmy Hollywood](/wiki/Jimmy_Hollywood "Jimmy Hollywood")* (1994), *[With Honors](/wiki/With_Honors_(film) "With Honors (film)")* (also 1994) and *[Gone Fishin'](/wiki/Gone_Fishin%27_(film) "Gone Fishin' (film)")* (1997). Pesci's role in *With Honors* was a dramatic role in which he played a homeless man living on the campus of Harvard.

### Musical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joe_Pesci&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Musical")]

In 1998, Pesci released his second album and his first in 30 years, *[Vincent LaGuardia Gambini Sings Just for You](/wiki/Vincent_LaGuardia_Gambini_Sings_Just_for_You "Vincent LaGuardia Gambini Sings Just for You"),* which was named after his character from the 1992 film *[My Cousin Vinny](/wiki/My_Cousin_Vinny "My Cousin Vinny")*. The album was both humorous and serious, exploring a variety of genres, though most of it was [big band](/wiki/Big_band "Big band") [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"). The album spawned the single "Wise Guy", a rap number that played on the [gangsta](/wiki/Gangsta_rap "Gangsta rap") theme by making reference to [Mafia](/wiki/American_Mafia "American Mafia") gangsterism. "Wise Guy" interpolated the 1980 hit "[Rapture](/wiki/Rapture_(Blondie_song) "Rapture (Blondie song)")" by [Blondie](/wiki/Blondie_(band) "Blondie (band)") and was co-written and produced by the hip-hop production team the [Trackmasters](/wiki/Trackmasters "Trackmasters").

### Semi-retirement from acting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joe_Pesci&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Semi-retirement from acting")]

In 1999, Pesci retired from acting to pursue a musical career and to enjoy life away from the camera. He returned to acting when he did a [cameo](/wiki/Cameo_appearance "Cameo appearance") in De Niro's 2006 film *[The Good Shepherd](/wiki/The_Good_Shepherd_(film) "The Good Shepherd (film)")*. In 2010, Pesci starred in the brothel drama *[Love Ranch](/wiki/Love_Ranch "Love Ranch")* alongside [Helen Mirren](/wiki/Helen_Mirren "Helen Mirren").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Pesci appeared with [Don Rickles](/wiki/Don_Rickles "Don Rickles"), with whom he had worked in *[Casino](/wiki/Casino_(1995_film) "Casino (1995 film)")*, in a 2011 [Snickers](/wiki/Snickers "Snickers") advertisement in which he portrays the angry alter ego of a young man who attends a party and becomes agitated by two women until he is calmed down by eating a Snickers bar.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In 2011, Pesci sued Fiore Films, the producers of the film *[Gotti](/wiki/Gotti_(2018_film) "Gotti (2018 film)")*, saying they had broken their promise to cast him in the film as real-life mobster [Angelo Ruggiero](/wiki/Angelo_Ruggiero "Angelo Ruggiero").[[26]](#cite_note-settles_dispute-26) Pesci stated that he had gained 30 pounds (14 kg) for the role.[[26]](#cite_note-settles_dispute-26) He sued them for $3 million, which was the payment he had been promised.[[26]](#cite_note-settles_dispute-26) The lawsuit was settled out of court in 2013 for an unspecified sum,[[26]](#cite_note-settles_dispute-26) and the role, after many production delays, eventually went to [Pruitt Taylor Vince](/wiki/Pruitt_Taylor_Vince "Pruitt Taylor Vince").

Pesci appears in the 2016 music documentary *Jimmy Scott: I Go Back Home,* in which he is filmed recording "[The Folks Who Live on the Hill](/wiki/The_Folks_Who_Live_on_the_Hill "The Folks Who Live on the Hill")" from Scott's 2017 posthumous album *I Go Back Home*.

### Reprise for *The Irishman* and subsequent roles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joe_Pesci&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Reprise for The Irishman and subsequent roles")]

In 2017, Pesci was cast alongside [Robert De Niro](/wiki/Robert_De_Niro "Robert De Niro") and [Al Pacino](/wiki/Al_Pacino "Al Pacino") in *[The Irishman](/wiki/The_Irishman "The Irishman")*, a crime film directed by [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese"). He was offered his role a reported 50 times before agreeing to take part, at first saying that he did not want to do "the gangster thing again," while Scorsese tried to persuade Pesci that *The Irishman* would be "different."[[27]](#cite_note-SpikeLee-27) The film received a [limited theatrical release](/wiki/Limited_theatrical_release "Limited theatrical release") on November 1, 2019, followed by digital streaming on November 27, 2019, by [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix").[[28]](#cite_note-ReleaseDate-28) Pesci's performance as [Russell Bufalino](/wiki/Russell_Bufalino "Russell Bufalino") was critically acclaimed and earned him various accolades, including nominations for the [Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor"),[[29]](#cite_note-29) the [BAFTA Award for Best Supporting Actor](/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor "BAFTA Award for Best Supporting Actor") and for two [Screen Actors Guild Awards](/wiki/Screen_Actors_Guild_Award "Screen Actors Guild Award"). Pesci also returned to music with his third album and his first in 21 years, titled *Pesci... Still Singing*, released on November 29, 2019.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Following *The Irishman*, Pesci co-starred in [Pete Davidson](/wiki/Pete_Davidson "Pete Davidson")'s comedy series *[Bupkis](/wiki/Bupkis_(TV_series) "Bupkis (TV series)")* and appeared in [Jack Huston](/wiki/Jack_Huston "Jack Huston")'s directorial debut *[Day of the Fight](/wiki/Day_of_the_Fight_(2023_film) "Day of the Fight (2023 film)")*, both released in 2023.

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joe_Pesci&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Pesci has been married and divorced three times.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

His first marriage was in January 1964.[[32]](#cite_note-32) His second marriage was from 1988 to 1992, to [Claudia Haro](/wiki/Claudia_Haro "Claudia Haro"), a model and actress. They have a daughter.[[33]](#cite_note-33) In 2000, Pesci started dating [Angie Everhart](/wiki/Angie_Everhart "Angie Everhart"). They became engaged in 2007 but broke up the following year.[[34]](#cite_note-34)
